sources.title: Why Do I Have to Take This Course? A Guide to General Education [Revised Edition]

tasks.chapterTitle: Part I: What is General Education?
Part VII: What About After Graduation?

messages.content:
This summary covers the core concepts of General Education as outlined in the provided text, designed to help students understand the purpose behind their curricular requirements.

### **Summary: Understanding General Education**

#### **What Is General Education?**
General education consists of the courses required for a degree outside of your specific major. These requirements are organized into a **curriculum**—a planned course of study designed to build academic, professional, civic, and personal skills.

#### **Key Terminology**
*   **Liberal Arts and Sciences:** Often used synonymously with "general education," this approach to learning is designed to empower individuals to navigate complexity and change. Its roots come from the Latin *liber* (free person) and *arts* (learned skills), meaning these are the skills deemed necessary for a "free person" to function effectively and joyfully in society.
*   **The Three Pillars of Curriculum:** Most modern programs are categorized into:
    *   **Humanities:** Literature, history, philosophy, arts, and languages.
    *   **Social Sciences:** Sociology, political, science, economics, etc.
    *   **STEM:** Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.

#### **Historical Context**
The concept of a "liberal education" dates back to ancient Greece and Rome, where it was initially intended to train citizens to participate in civic life [S.29, p. 13]. Over centuries, the classification of these subjects has evolved—from the medieval **trivium** (grammar, logic, rhetoric) and **quadrivium** (arithmetic, geometry, music, astronomy) to modern academic structures [S.29, p. 13]. Despite these shifts, the core goal remains consistent: providing students with a comprehensive foundation to "comprehend everything they read" and "cut through the knots of all problems" [S.30, p. 14].

#### **Types of Institutions**
General education requirements vary based on the mission and type of institution, including:
*   **Universities vs. Community Colleges:** Differences in degree levels (bachelor/graduate vs. associate/certificates) [S.31, p. 15].
*   **Public vs. Private:** Differences in funding sources and tuition structures [S.31, p. 15].
*   **Specialized Institutions:** Some schools, such as liberal arts colleges, have specific missions that center their entire curriculum around this broad-based approach [S.31, p. 15].

***

**Tutor's Note:** Understanding that these courses are intentional—meant to sharpen your discernment and intellect—can help transform them from a "checkbox" requirement into a tool for your professional and personal success [S.28, p. 12].

Lesson 1: Why Do I Have to Take This Course - Presentation7 slides
Why Do I Have to Take This Course?
  • Understand the purpose of general education beyond just earning a degree.
  • Explore how these courses develop essential personal, civic, and professional skills.

This book, *Why Do I Have to Take This Course?* by Kisha G. Tracy ([S.3]), is designed to help students understand the purpose and importance of general education requirements in higher education ([S.17]).

**Key Points**

**Purpose of the Text**
* Many students experience confusion, apathy, or resentment toward general education requirements because their purpose is often misunderstood or poorly communicated by institutions ([S.17]).
* The book serves as a resource to foster conversations about the value of these courses, aiming to help students engage more deeply with their curriculum ([S.17]).

**Structure of the Book**
* The content is organized into four thematic areas:
    * What is general education? (History and curriculum design) ([S.17]).
    * Why general education? (Overarching programs and personal development) ([S.17]).
    * What will I learn? (Significance of specific learning outcomes and "good, necessary trouble") ([S.17]).
    * What about after graduation? (The role of education in life beyond college) ([S.17]).
* Additional sections are provided specifically for instructors to improve how they communicate the significance of their courses to students ([S.17], [S.18]).

**Authorial Context**
* Kisha G. Tracy is a Professor of English Studies at Fitchburg State University with a background in Medieval Studies ([S.19]).
* Her teaching philosophy incorporates anti-racist pedagogy, universal design for learning, and trauma-informed instruction ([S.19]).
* She integrates her personal passion for photography into her work to build connections with students and bridge gaps in cultural empathy and historical understanding ([S.21]).

**Institutional Recognition**
* The text includes formal land and labor acknowledgments, recognizing the Indigenous Peoples of Massachusetts and the history of systemic injustice and slavery that shaped the region ([S.22], [S.23]).

General education serves as a foundational component of collegiate study, distinct from one’s specific major. This document outlines the fundamental concepts, history, and structural development of these requirements.

### Key Terminology
General education refers to required courses designed to develop academic, personal, civic, and professional skills [S.27]. It is often used interchangeably with "liberal arts and sciences," an approach to learning that prepares individuals for complex, changing environments by fostering intellectual flexibility and social responsibility [S.27, S.28]. Modern curriculum is typically organized into three primary domains of knowledge:
1. The Humanities: Including literature, history, philosophy, and the arts [S.28].
2. Social Sciences: Including sociology, political science, and economics [S.28].
3. STEM: Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics [S.28].

### Historical Context
The concept of liberal arts has roots in classical antiquity. Ancient Greek and Roman societies valued these studies as essential training for free citizens to participate in civic life [S.29]. During the Middle Ages, the curriculum was organized into the trivium (grammar, logic, and rhetoric) and the quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, music, and astronomy) [S.29]. While definitions have evolved from a focus on the "free person" to a broader emphasis on human wisdom and problem-solving, the core objective—providing a comprehensive breadth of knowledge—remains central to the discipline [S.29, S.30].

### Institutional Structure and Governance
Higher education institutions vary by mission:
* Universities offer a wide range of degrees, whereas community and vocational colleges focus on specific career paths or two-year programs [S.31, S.32].
* Liberal arts institutions prioritize a broad curriculum alongside career preparation [S.32].

Curricula are designed by faculty and administrators, but must often adhere to accreditation standards [S.32]. Agencies, such as the New England Commission of Higher Education (NECHE), require institutions to maintain a "coherent and substantive" general education program that ensures a balanced exposure to the arts/humanities, sciences, and social sciences [S.33]. Ultimately, general education seeks to prepare graduates to manage not only their professional responsibilities but also their lives outside of work [S.34].

This text explains why a general education curriculum is a fundamental part of higher education rather than mere "busy work."

The value of a liberal arts education is established through several key themes:

Reframing Perspective
Students often dismiss required courses as checkboxes. However, actively engaging with these courses—rather than treating them as "busy work"—increases knowledge retention. Self-reflection and an awareness of one's own learning process are critical strategies for applying skills to new, unpredictable situations [S.39].

The Transformative Power of Education
A liberal arts education exposes students to diverse ideas and populations, which can challenge deeply held biases. The case of Derek Black demonstrates that this exposure can help individuals critically evaluate their own beliefs and replace harmful ideologies with a rigorous pursuit of truth [S.40].

The Importance of Breadth
While individuals could technically learn subjects on their own, the general education curriculum provides a deliberate, comprehensive foundation guided by experts. This structure allows students to encounter fields they might not have explored otherwise and enables them to "learn how to learn" from multiple perspectives [S.42].

Developing a "Toolbox" for Life
College is a unique, safe space to develop skills like critical thinking, communication, digital literacy, and quantitative reasoning. These skills form a "toolbox of knowledge" that prepares individuals for various future roles—as students, citizens, and professionals. When faced with unexpected global challenges, such as a pandemic, this diverse background of knowledge allows individuals to navigate complex ethical, scientific, and social issues more effectively [S.47, S.49, S.50].

The Role of Failure
Progress in learning is rarely linear. Adopting a perspective similar to the Japanese art of Kintsugi—which views broken, repaired objects as beautiful—helps students understand that mistakes and setbacks are essential parts of the development process. By applying feedback and learning from failure, students strengthen their intellectual growth [S.48].

This section introduces the mission of general education programs and connects the concept of academic preparation to social activism. The core elements are summarized below:

**General Education Mission**
The Fitchburg State University General Education program aims to foster creative and critical thinkers. The curriculum is designed to help students integrate knowledge from various disciplines to enhance their personal, civic, and professional lives. Learning outcomes are established at the start of courses to define the specific skills and knowledge students are expected to acquire through study and practice [S.73].

**John Lewis and "Good, Necessary Trouble"**
John Lewis was a pivotal civil rights leader, activist, and long-serving U.S. Representative. He was a key figure in the Civil Rights Movement, involved in the Nashville Student Movement, the Freedom Riders, and the 1963 March on Washington [S.77, S.78]. Lewis famously advocated for "good, necessary trouble"—a term that does not advocate for chaos or recklessness, but rather for purposeful action and persistent effort to effect positive societal change [S.78]. He emphasized that participating in the democratic process, particularly voting and education, serves as a powerful instrument for nonviolent progress [S.78].

**The Intersection of Education and Activism**
The text posits that academic learning and social change are deeply interconnected. Effective activism—or "good, necessary trouble"—is not merely spontaneous; it requires intense preparation, strategic thinking, and a firm grasp of history [S.80]. General education provides the vital foundation students need to:
1. Develop the empathy and knowledge required to understand complex human issues [S.80].
2. Hone the skills necessary to argue, organize, and strategize against systemic opposition [S.80].
3. Increase the overall impact of their work through rigorous study [S.80].

Ultimately, the text suggests that by applying the lessons of general education to real-world struggles, individuals can better contribute to the "long-term existential struggle" of improving humanity [S.78, S.80].

This document provides an overview of two essential foundational skills: Information Literacy and Quantitative Reasoning.

### Information Literacy
Information literacy is defined as the ability to identify, locate, evaluate, and use information effectively and ethically. It is a critical skill for navigating a world saturated with social media, news, and data.

**Key Components:**
* **Identification:** Recognizing what information is needed to solve a problem or build an informed opinion.
* **Evaluation:** Questioning the credibility, purpose, bias, and accuracy of a source. This is crucial for distinguishing between factual reporting, misinformation, disinformation, and propaganda.
* **Ethical Usage:** Ensuring that sources are properly credited and that information is not deliberately misrepresented (p. 86-88).
* **Societal Impact:** Misinformation has tangible consequences, including financial loss in consumer markets and increased social or emotional distress during public health crises (p. 90). Developing these skills is framed as a responsibility of citizenship and a way to participate meaningfully in communities (p. 88).

### Quantitative Reasoning
Quantitative reasoning involves analyzing and interpreting mathematical information to make informed choices. Rather than just performing calculations, it serves as a tool for thinking and communication (p. 92).

**Key Components:**
* **Problem-Solving:** It enables individuals to draw inferences, assess risks, interpret models, and test hypotheses in complex real-world situations (p. 92).
* **Social and Civic Applications:** Quantitative literacy acts as a "mirror and a lens" to examine sociopolitical issues, such as income inequality, crime, healthcare, and educational funding (p. 93).
* **Broad Applicability:** Beyond academic research, these skills are required for personal finance, career planning, and evaluating the validity of news data. It is a fundamental requirement for functioning effectively as an informed citizen in a data-driven society (p. 93).

Both fields emphasize that these skills are not limited to the classroom but are essential for navigating the complexities of modern life and acting as responsible members of society.

This document covers two main components of an Exploration-based general education curriculum: Civic Learning and Diverse Perspectives.

### Civic Learning (Pages S.129-S.135)
Civic Learning prepares students to be informed, engaged citizens capable of participating in democratic processes. Its core objective is to move beyond mere voting toward an understanding of rights, responsibilities, and the ability to influence public institutions.

Key dimensions of Civic Learning include:
- Self: Developing identity and reflective practice.
- Communities and Cultures: Cultivating empathy and recognizing systemic inequalities.
- Knowledge: Developing information literacy and understanding democratic principles.
- Skills: Mastering conflict resolution, deliberation, and collaborative problem-solving.
- Values: Applying ethical reasoning to support justice and liberty.
- Public Action: Engaging in democratic processes and social advocacy.

The text emphasizes that these skills are highly transferable to the 21st-century workplace. It also uses the history of the women’s suffrage movement and the evolution of affirmative action in higher education (from *Regents of the University of California v. Bakke* to the 2023 Supreme Court rulings) to illustrate the importance of "good, necessary trouble"—active, principled engagement to address systemic challenges.

### Diverse Perspectives (Pages S.136-S.138)
The study of Diverse Perspectives encourages students to critically examine how different cultural and intellectual frameworks shape social reality. By engaging with histories and practices different from their own, students can reflect on their own "invisible" cultural assumptions.

Key takeaways regarding diversity:
- Diversity Enriches Humanity: Recognizing variations in culture, biology, and belief leads to more complex and creative thinking.
- Campus Benefits: Exposure to diversity matures a student's worldview and fosters personal responsibility in standing against prejudice.
- Conflict Resolution: Developing respect, open-mindedness, and a willingness to compromise is essential when navigating a world characterized by diverse viewpoints.
- Moving Beyond Stereotypes: The goal of this exploration is not to generalize groups of people but to appreciate the unique backgrounds and behaviors of individuals, ultimately leading to greater social cohesion, as demonstrated by historical examples like the welcoming community of Geel, Belgium.

This section focuses on the development of three essential skills beyond foundational academics: Creative Thinking, Critical Thinking, and Digital Literacy.

### Creative Thinking
Creative thinking is the ability to synthesize existing ideas in original ways and engage in innovation. It is defined as a process that generates valuable ideas and is applicable in all areas of life, not just the arts. Developing this skill helps students adapt to a rapidly changing world and fosters personal and professional growth (p. [S.197]).

### Critical Thinking
Critical thinking is described as a self-directed and disciplined habit of exploring ideas, evidence, and artifacts before concluding. It involves:
- Raising clear questions and problems (p. [S.199]).
- Assessing information effectively and testing conclusions against rigorous standards (p. [S.199]).
- Assuming an open-minded approach within diverse systems of thought (p. [S.199]).
- Improving the quality of thought by taking charge of inherent thinking structures (p. [S.198]).

### Digital Literacy
Digital literacy involves using information and communication technologies to find, evaluate, create, and share information. Key aspects include:
- Facility with digital tools and platforms for communication and collaboration (p. [S.200]).
- The ability to distinguish reliable sources from misinformation, thereby nurturing critical thinking (p. [S.200]).
- Ensuring online safety, privacy, and understanding digital threats (p. [S.200]).
- Enhancing employability and facilitating active participation in democratic processes (p. [S.200]).

### Conclusion
These skills collectively act as a "toolbox" for students. By applying these methods of inquiry and problem-solving, individuals are better equipped to interpret complex experiences, address professional challenges, and navigate diverse social environments (p. [S.202]).

This section focuses on preparing students for life after graduation, emphasizing personal growth, continuous learning, and professional articulation of academic skills.

**How to Human (Effectively!) 102**
Students are encouraged to reflect on their general education curriculum to identify the specific skills and knowledge acquired that are necessary for functioning effectively as human beings. This involves connecting past coursework to broader life competencies [S.235].

**Lifelong Learning**
Lifelong learning is defined as the ongoing, purposeful pursuit of knowledge and personal growth. It is essential for adaptability in a changing workforce and society. Key takeaways include:
* It is a transformative process that spans a person's entire lifetime [S.237].
* A liberal arts education provides the flexibility needed to navigate multiple career paths [S.237-S.238].
* It is considered a human right that fosters empathy and promotes peaceful communication across diverse groups [S.238].

**Action Planning**
To maximize the value of their education, students should utilize tools like the General Education Action Plan. Engaging with academic advisors helps students align their collegiate activities with potential life and career aspirations [S.239].

**"Selling" General Education**
Students must learn to translate their academic experiences into professional terms. This involves:
* Conceptualizing coursework as real-world skills (e.g., transforming a literature analysis project into evidence of research and communication capability) [S.241].
* Creating a "General Education résumé" that highlights the transferable skills gained beyond the major, making students more competitive for future employers [S.241-S.242].

**Conclusion**
The ultimate goal of a degree is to equip graduates with the mindset to embrace learning as a continuous journey. Reflection at the end of the academic experience is crucial to understanding the personal growth achieved throughout the process [S.243].

This text, intended for instructors, discusses strategies for fostering student engagement and empathy in higher education.

**Addressing the Question of Relevance**
When students ask, "Why do I have to take this course?" they are often expressing skepticism regarding the value of general education. Drawing on Queen Elizabeth I's "Speech to the Troops at Tilbury," the text suggests that instructors should provide multiple, diverse reasons for students to invest in their learning. Just as Elizabeth appealed to faith, patriotism, and financial rewards to motivate her soldiers, instructors must tailor their approaches to reach students with different backgrounds and interests (p. 230-231).

**The Expert Blind Spot**
The author notes that instructors often experience an "expert blind spot," where they struggle to relate to the needs of novice learners because they have forgotten what it is like to be a beginner in their field (p. 231). Bridging this gap requires instructors to intentionally design their teaching to foster investment, rather than assuming students intuitively understand the value of the curriculum (p. 231-232).

**Acknowledging Student Realities**
A significant portion of the text emphasizes that students are adults facing complex, real-world challenges. Statistical data highlights that many students deal with food insecurity, poverty, employment requirements, parenthood, mental health struggles, and learning disabilities (p. 234-235). The brain’s capacity for learning is significantly diminished under stress, making these external factors direct obstacles to academic success (p. 236).

**Empathy and Human Connection**
The text argues that instructors and students share more commonalities than acknowledged, including the impact of stress on performance. Professors are encouraged to move beyond "dualistic thinking" and recognize that questioning is a legitimate stage of intellectual development. By treating students with respect and acknowledging their diverse life circumstances, instructors can create an environment that encourages students to move from merely "showing up" to becoming fully invested in their education (p. 232-233, 235-236).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the text, what is the primary purpose of defining the purpose and goals of general education for students?
options: To meet the legal requirements of accreditation boards.
To ensure students select a major faster.
To justify the cost of tuition for undergraduate degrees.
To reduce student apathy and resentment, leading to better engagement.
questions.explanation: The author notes that when students do not know the reason why they are doing something, it can lead to apathy and resentment. Understanding the purpose helps them appreciate and learn from the course.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes 'general education courses' as defined in the text?
options: Courses taken exclusively within a student's chosen major.
Courses that are only required during a student's final year.
Required courses for university students outside of their specific major.
All requirements for a degree, including major and elective courses.
questions.explanation: The text defines general education courses as 'all those required for college and university students to take besides those required in a major.'

questions.question: Why does the author, Kisha G. Tracy, include her own background and photographic work in this book?
options: To document the historical sites of Massachusetts for academic credit.
To demonstrate that she is a professional photographer by trade.
To fulfill the personal requirements of the ROTEL grant.
To connect her expertise and passions with her pedagogy to engage students.
questions.explanation: The author explains that bringing her passions, like photography, into the classroom allows her to speak to students in unique ways and bridge gaps in geography, time, and cultural empathy.

questions.question: What is the main goal of the 'Land Acknowledgement' provided in the text?
options: To provide a list of local tourism sites for students to visit.
To acknowledge the Indigenous Peoples as traditional stewards of the land and honor their history.
To grant the university full ownership of the land where it is located.
To outline the current legal boundaries of the state of Massachusetts.
questions.explanation: The land acknowledgement aims to recognize Indigenous Peoples as traditional stewards of the land and to acknowledge the painful history of genocide and forced removal connected with colonization.

questions.question: How does the author suggest instructors should use this book?
options: It is primarily for high school teachers rather than university instructors.
It is intended solely as an independent study guide for students without teacher guidance.
It should only be read in its entirety in a specific order.
It can be assigned in its entirety or focused on specific sections relevant to course outcomes.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'For instructors who wish to adopt this book, please note that it is designed to assign in its entirety or, more likely, in parts.'

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of the book 'Why Do I Have to Take This Course?'
cards.back: To explain the significance, purpose, and goals of general education courses to students and provide a resource for discussions across campus.

cards.front: What are defined as General Education courses?
cards.back: General education courses are all those required for college and university students beyond those specifically required in their major.
A curriculum is the collection of all courses or requirements that make up a specific course of study.

cards.front: Who is the author of 'Why Do I Have to Take This Course?'
What is the primary academic field of the author, Kisha G. Tracy?
How does the author characterize the feeling of students who do not understand the reason behind course requirements?
cards.back: A lack of understanding can lead to apathy and even resentment.

cards.front: Which group of people does the 'Land Acknowledgement' specifically recognize?
cards.back: Indigenous Peoples as the traditional stewards of the land and their enduring relationship to their territories.

cards.front: What is the significance of the term 'Good, Necessary Trouble' in this text?
cards.back: It refers to the concept popularized by John Lewis, used here to frame the purpose and challenges of learning in general education.

cards.front: What are the four main sections of the book 'Why Do I Have to Take This Course?'
cards.back: 1. What is general education? 2. Why general education? 3. What will I learn? 4. What about after graduation?

cards.front: How does the author utilize photography in her teaching and research?
cards.back: The author uses photography as a unique communication tool to bridge gaps in geography, time, and cultural empathy while connecting cultural heritage with literature studies.

slides.title: Why Do I Have to Take This Course?
bullets: Understand the purpose of general education beyond just earning a degree.
Explore how these courses develop essential personal, civic, and professional skills.
slides.notes: Welcome to your guide on making sense of general education. We will look at why these requirements are foundational to your success.

slides.title: What is General Education?
bullets: Courses required outside of your chosen major (p. 27).
A curriculum designed to develop personal, civic, and professional skills (p. 27).
Liberal arts approach: empowering individuals to handle complexity and change (p. 28).
Broadens general knowledge, deriving from Latin 'liber' for 'free person' (p. 28).
slides.notes: General education is more than just extra classes; it's a 'degree in thinking' that helps you live effectively.

slides.title: Why General Education Matters
bullets: Moves beyond 'busy work' to develop transferable intellectual skills (p. 39).
Promotes self-reflection, helping you retain learning longer (p. 39).
Provides exposure to diverse people and ideas to challenge preconceptions (p. 40).
Equips you to recognize and question social roles in the 'cycle of socialization' (p. 40).
slides.notes: These courses act as a catalyst for growth, helping you identify your own values and understand the wider world.

slides.title: The Core Mission
bullets: General education aims to build critical and creative thinkers (p. 57).
Integration of learning from various disciplines across three areas (p. 58).
Learn to navigate 'good, necessary trouble' inspired by activists like John Lewis (p. 62).
Uses democratic participation to advance positive social change (p. 62).
slides.notes: Your education isn't just about facts; it's about preparing you to be an active, critical participant in a complex society.

slides.title: Developing Foundation Skills
bullets: Info Literacy: Locate, evaluate, and use information effectively and ethically (p. 69).
Critical Thinking: Systematically improving the quality of our thoughts (p. 182).
Digital Literacy: Navigating tools to solve problems and communicate effectively (p. 200).
These skills underpin success in all other academic and professional areas (p. 69).
slides.notes: These foundational skills are the tools you use to parse the massive amount of information you encounter daily.

slides.title: Exploration and Civic Learning
bullets: Exploration courses provide diverse viewpoints and multiple methods of thinking (p. 129).
Civic Learning prepares you for participation in democratic life (p. 130).
Focuses on consensus building, problem-solving, and valuing diverse cultures (p. 130-131).
Essential for both modern workplaces and informed engaged citizenship (p. 130).
slides.notes: Civic learning bridges the gap between your studies and the real-world responsibilities of being an effective citizen.

slides.title: After Graduation: Lifelong Learning
bullets: General education prepares you for a flexible, lifelong learning path (p. 221).
Learning is essential for navigating multiple careers over a lifetime (p. 221).
Translate academic experiences into real-world professional vocabulary (p. 241).
Continuously adapt to new challenges with confidence and curiosity (p. 221).
slides.notes: College is only the start; the most important lesson is learning how to keep learning long after you have your degree.
